text: Hello. This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: [0] 你好。这里是BBC英语学习的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Neil. And I'm Bekka. Neil, are there any foods that you used to really hate in the past, but now don't mind?
zh: [1] 我是尼尔。我是贝卡。尼尔,有没有一些你以前很讨厌、但现在却不介意的食物?

text: Yes, actually. There's a Japanese food called umeboshi,
zh: [2] 有的,还真有。有一种日本食物叫梅干,

text: which when I first tried it, it I really didn't like, but after a while I got used to it and actually now I really love it.
zh: [3] 我第一次尝的时候真的不喜欢,但过了一段时间就习惯了,现在我其实非常喜欢它。

text: How about you? A similar story to me with olives.
zh: [4] 你呢?我和你的情况类似,是橄榄。

text: I used to really hate olives, but as I've grown older I've also grown to love them. Well,
zh: [5] 我以前很讨厌橄榄,但随着年龄增长,我也渐渐爱上了它们。好了,

text: in this episode we'll be learning from food experts about why there are some foods we just hate and whether it's possible to learn to love them.
zh: [6] 在这一期节目中,我们将向美食专家学习,了解为什么我们会讨厌某些食物,以及是否有可能学会喜欢上它们。

text: Yes, and as usual you can find a transcript for this episode,
zh: [7] 是的,和往常一样,你可以找到这一期的文字稿,

text: along with all the vocabulary and a worksheet on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: [8] 还有所有词汇和一份练习表,都在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上。

text: OK, the question for you, Bekka. What word means to have a fear of new things, such as trying new foods?
zh: [9] 好,贝卡,给你的问题是:哪个词表示对新事物,比如尝试新食物,感到害怕?

text: Is it a aerophobia, b claustrophobia, or c neophobia?
zh: [10] 是a. aerophobia(恐飞症),b. claustrophobia(幽闭恐惧症),还是c. neophobia(新事物恐惧症)?

text: Well, Nil, I'll go for c neophobia, because neo sounds like new.
zh: [11] 尼尔,我选c,neophobia,因为neo听起来像new(新)。

text: That's clever thinking, but let's see. Now though, back to food.
zh: [12] 这个思路很巧妙,不过我们稍后揭晓答案。现在回到食物的话题。

text: Some experts have said that we can teach ourselves to like new things.
zh: [13] 一些专家表示,我们可以训练自己去喜欢新事物。

text: Ruth Alexander asks Dr Dana Small of McGill University where our dislike of certain foods comes from in this BBC World Service programme, The Food Chain.
zh: [14] 在BBC世界服务的节目《食物链》中,露丝·亚历山大向麦吉尔大学的达娜·斯莫尔博士请教,我们对某些食物的厌恶从何而来。

text: Are food dislikes learned or genetic, hardwired in some way?
zh: [15] 对食物的厌恶是后天习得的,还是基因决定、天生固有的?

text: Both. So there's many reasons why you can dislike a food.
zh: [16] 两者都有。你不喜欢一种食物可能有很多原因。

text: For example, you could via genetics smell coriander or taste coriander differently. So that's genetic,
zh: [17] 比如,由于基因差异,你闻到或尝到香菜的味道可能和别人不一样。这就是基因因素,

text: but there's also a really strong learning component.
zh: [18] 但同时也存在一个非常强烈的后天学习成分。

text: Dana explains that how we taste or smell something can be different depending on our genetics.
zh: [19] 达娜解释说,我们对某种东西的味觉或嗅觉感受可能因基因不同而有差异。

text: However, how we taste or smell something can also be learnt.
zh: [20] 不过,我们对某种东西的味觉或嗅觉感受也可能是后天学到的。

text: Ruth asked if a dislike for certain food is hardwired, and Dana confirmed that this is sometimes the case.
zh: [21] 露丝问对某些食物的厌恶是否是天生固有的,达娜确认有时确实如此。

text: The adjective hardwired describes automatically thinking or behaving in a particular way, for instance because it's
zh: [22] 形容词hardwired(天生固有的)形容一种自动化的思维或行为方式,比如因为基因原因。

text: genetic. And Dana says that you could taste food differently to others via genetics.
zh: [23] 达娜说,由于基因不同,你尝到的食物味道可能和别人不一样。

text: The preposition via means by the way of or by the use of. For example, I get to work via a train.
zh: [24] 介词via意思是“通过”或“借助”。比如,我通过坐火车去上班。

text: So there are lots of different reasons why we dislike some foods, but can we change that?
zh: [25] 所以我们不喜欢某些食物有很多不同的原因,但我们能改变这一点吗?

text: Let's listen to dietitian Claire Thornton -Wood explaining on the BBC World Service programme, The Food Chain.
zh: [26] 我们来听听营养师克莱尔·索恩顿-伍德在BBC世界服务节目《食物链》中的解释。

text: One of the really good techniques that we might use is something called masking,
zh: [27] 我们常用的一个非常有效的技巧叫做“遮蔽法”,

text: where you dip a food that you don't like into something that you do like.
zh: [28] 就是把你不喜欢的食物蘸上你喜欢的东西。

text: For those parents who really say they like everything, we actually get chocolate covered insects
zh: [29] 对于那些声称自己什么都喜欢吃的家长,我们其实会拿出巧克力包裹的昆虫,

text: and we offer those and actually people do eat them and try them.
zh: [30] 让他们尝试,结果人们真的会吃,也真的会去尝。

text: And I think it's the concept that it's an insect,
zh: [31] 我觉得关键在于“这是昆虫”这个概念本身让人却步,

text: but usually they find that once they eat it, there isn't anything inherently unpleasant about it.
zh: [32] 但通常他们吃了之后会发现,其实并没有什么本质上令人不快的地方。

text: It's a little bit like eating just a bit of crunchy chocolate.
zh: [33] 吃起来有点像在吃一块酥脆的巧克力。

text: Claire uses Masking is the act of stopping something from being seen.
zh: [34] 克莱尔所说的masking(遮蔽)是指让某样东西不被看见的行为。

text: In Claire's clinic, she masks the disliked foods with something that is liked.
zh: [35] 在克莱尔的诊所里,她用喜欢的食物来“遮蔽”不喜欢的食物。

text: Yes, and what parents usually find out is that the food they dislike isn't inherently bad.
zh: [36] 是的,家长们通常会发现,他们不喜欢的食物本质上并不差。

text: The adverb inherently describes something that exists in a way which is natural or essential.
zh: [37] 副词inherently形容某事物本质上、天生自然存在的状态。

text: So insects aren't inherently unpleasant to eat.
zh: [38] 所以昆虫本质上并不难吃。

text: Some of us think they are because the concept of eating them could be strange to us culturally.
zh: [39] 我们有些人觉得难吃,是因为从文化角度看,吃昆虫这个概念对我们来说很陌生。

text: We talked about how people sometimes fear trying new food.
zh: [40] 我们谈到人们有时害怕尝试新食物。

text: Clare talks about where these fears might come from. For instance, just say that you had eaten prawns
zh: [41] 克莱尔谈到这些恐惧可能的来源。比如说,假设你以前吃过虾,

text: in the past and you had become unwell from eating them, you know, you'd had what you call a dodgy prawn.
zh: [42] 结果吃完之后身体不适,也就是所谓吃到了“有问题的虾”。

text: There's a good chance that you might actually associate that with eating the prawn and
zh: [43] 那么你很可能会把这次不适和吃虾联系在一起,

text: think, oh, I don't want to eat the prawn again because it's going to make me unwell. So that's the sort of fear -based avoidance.
zh: [44] 心想:“哦,我不想再吃虾了,因为它会让我不舒服。”这就是一种基于恐惧的回避行为。

text: Claire said that you're likely to have a fear of a food if you've had a bad experience with it.
zh: [45] 克莱尔说,如果你曾经因为某种食物有过糟糕的经历,你就很可能会对它产生恐惧。

text: She uses the example of eating a dodgy prawn, which would make you unwell.
zh: [46] 她举了吃到“有问题的虾”导致身体不适的例子。

text: If something is dodgy, it's generally bad or has a bad reputation.
zh: [47] 如果某样东西是dodgy(靠不住的),通常意味着它不好,或名声不佳。

text: But when we talk about food, it could mean that it's undercooked, old or has been left out, therefore making you sick.
zh: [48] 但说到食物时,它可能意味着没煮熟、变质,或放置太久,从而让你生病。

text: In fact, we may avoid dodgy things. To avoid is to keep away from something.
zh: [49] 事实上,我们可能会避开这些“靠不住”的东西。avoid(避免)就是远离某样东西。

text: So, avoidance is the act of keeping away. Now, Neil, that reminds me of the question you asked earlier.
zh: [50] 所以,avoidance(回避)就是远离的行为。尼尔,这让我想起你之前提的那个问题。

text: Ah yes, I asked you what word means to have a fear of new things and you answered C, neophobia. phobia.
zh: [51] 啊对,我问的是哪个词表示害怕新事物,你回答的是c,neophobia。

text: And, Bekka, I'm pleased to say your answer was correct.
zh: [52] 贝卡,我很高兴地告诉你,你答对了。

text: So my thinking was right! That's great. Now let's have a recap of the language we've learnt in this episode, starting with
zh: [53] 那我的推理是对的!太好了。现在我们来回顾一下这期节目学到的词汇,先从

text: hardwired, which describes automatically thinking or behaving in a particular way, because of genetics, for example.
zh: [54] hardwired开始,它形容一种自动化的思维或行为方式,比如是由基因决定的。

text: Via is a preposition that means by way of or by use of.
zh: [55] via是一个介词,意思是“通过”或“借助”。

text: We had masking, that's hiding or stopping something from being seen.
zh: [56] 还有masking,指的是隐藏或阻止某物被看见。

text: The adverb inherently describes something that exists in a way which is natural or essential.
zh: [57] 副词inherently形容某事物本质上、天生自然存在的状态。

text: We also had avoidance. That is the act of keeping away from something.
zh: [58] 我们还学了avoidance,指远离某事物的行为。

text: And finally, when we talk about food, dodgy means something that can make you unwell.
zh: [59] 最后,在谈到食物时,dodgy指可能让你生病的东西。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but head over to our website bbclearningenglish .com for a quiz and worksheet for this episode.
zh: [60] 我们的六分钟又到了,不过别忘了访问我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com,获取本期的测验和练习表。

text: See you there soon, but for now, it's Goodbye!
zh: [61] 我们很快在那里见,不过现在,再见啦!

text: English from bbclearningenglish .com
zh: [62] 英语学习,来自bbclearningenglish.com
